ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bee Hoon Goreng; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03311069) as the most defensible ancestor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated code for Bee Hoon Goreng; A0EJW (stir-fried noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category anchor available in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Goreng with Meat weighs approximately 400 g, representing a typical single-person hawker or restaurant serving in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Fried Bee Hoon with Meat
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore nutrient composition database entry for fried bee hoon with meat used as the primary reference. Input values from the GrabFood-derived record (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against HPB data for fried bee hoon with meat and found consistent. HPB-aligned values are used; minor rounding applied. Potassium estimated at 210 mg/100g based on typical vegetable and meat composition in this dish class; not directly reported by HPB for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Goreng with Meat. GI of 58 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli / fried rice noodles (GI ~58, medium category), as documented in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values. The mixed dish contains rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source; protein and fat from meat and egg moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (400 g × 17.8 g/100g = 71.2 g) / 100 = 41.3, rounded to 41.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100g; estimated 1600 mg per standard 400 g plate
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving provides approximately 1600 mg sodium — 70% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — driven by dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, kecap manis, and tomato ketchup; patients with hypertension should request reduced sauces or limit portion to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (17.8 g/100g) with medium GI (~58); 400 g serving delivers ~71 g carbohydrate and GL ~41
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of rice vermicelli is partially offset by protein (6.7 g/100g) and fat (7.8 g/100g) from meat and egg, but the large standard portion (400 g) produces a high glycaemic load of ~41; patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to 200–250 g and request reduced kecap manis to lower added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.2 g/100g, sodium 400 mg/100g, cholesterol 40 mg/100g; trans fat present at 0.1 g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (8.8 g per 400 g serving), trans fat (0.4 g per serving), and high sodium (1600 mg per serving) is unfavourable for cardiovascular health; patients with CVD should choose chicken over red meat, request reduced sauces, and limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef/mutton) is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways; minimal anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: If prepared with beef or mutton, this dish is unsuitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to high arachidonic acid content; specify chicken, request reduced soy and oyster sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling, and monitor nightshade sensitivity from chilli paste and tomato sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Goreng with Meat is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean stir-fried noodle dish made from rice vermicelli (bee hoon) cooked over high heat in a wok with a choice of meat — typically chicken, beef, or mutton — along with egg, cabbage, bean sprouts, and a flavourful sauce base of dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), tomato ketchup, and chilli paste. The dish has a savoury, slightly sweet, and mildly spicy flavour profile with a soft yet slightly chewy noodle texture and caramelised wok-hei aroma from high-heat stir-frying. It is a staple at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and mamak stalls, commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 167 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 17.8 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg/100g — a clinically significant level for hypertension management. The medium glycaemic index (~58) and moderate carbohydrate content make portion control important for type 2 diabetes patients. The dish is typically halal-certified when prepared at Muslim-operated stalls. It is also known as Mee Hoon Goreng, Bihun Goreng, or Fried Rice Vermicelli with Meat across the region.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bee hoon goreng with meat?
Is bee hoon goreng suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of bee hoon goreng?
Fried rice vermicelli with meat calories
Is bee hoon goreng good for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are cross-referenced against HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for fried bee hoon with meat and are consistent with the input GrabFood-derived record (confidence 0.7). No exact HPB entry title was available for this specific dish variant; values are adapted from the closest HPB match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli. Potassium is estimated from typical dish composition. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stir-fried bee hoon dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported for this dish in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail not available in HPB Singapore nutrient composition database for this dish class as of 2024. Potassium estimated at 210 mg/100g from typical vegetable and meat composition; not directly reported. The input record listed three portions all labelled '1 plate' with different weights (350 g, 400 g, 450 g); the 400 g value is used as the default serving size as it is the median and most commonly cited for this dish class. The 'Dosa' cuisine tag in the input is attributed to the restaurant offering multiple cuisines and is not a property of this dish. Country of origin listed as Singapore in the input; this dish is of Malaysian origin and is also a Singaporean hawker staple — region_of_origin set to 'Malaysia / Singapore'. No zero-value core macros are present in this record. Iteration 3 fixes: (1) nutrient_source URL corrected from foodo.sg (forbidden delivery/app-adjacent URL) to the official HPB nutrient composition page; (2) foodon_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; (3) foodex2_match_type corrected from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' — both now use only the permitted enum values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.3), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (2.2), sugar_g (3.3) all present as numeric values and plausible for this dish class.

GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate for rice vermicelli; estimation basis documented. gi_source organisation correctly set to 'University of Sydney GI Database'.
